sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presents with a non-healing wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, which he reports has been present for 2-3 weeks.
He denies any trauma to the foot and states that the wound was caused by a blister from a new pair of Hoka shoes.
He has been applying a band-aid and taking antibiotics as prescribed by his PCP, which he completed yesterday.
He denies any pain or discomfort in the wound and reports that it does not hurt when touched.
He also denies any other symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage from the wound.
He is wearing a different pair of shoes now and is upset about losing his Hoka shoes.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with an HbA1c of 8.1, and has seen a podiatrist in the past, but they moved and he has not been able to get another one.
He has neuropathy.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.
Physical exam: There is a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, just proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, and no necrosis.
There is no odor.
The vascular exam shows palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses, with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Diabetes mellitus; Peripheral neuropathy; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I prescribed Collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing, and ordered an Ankle Brachial Index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I performed a debridement of the wound in the office.
I want the patient to wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
I do not think any more antibiotics are needed at this time.
I want to see the patient again in two weeks and may refer him to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply.
I will also refer him to a diabetic specialist to help him control his diabetes.
Follow-up: I want to see the patient again in two weeks.
